title: The Shang dynasty consolidates in the Yellow River valley
summary: Around 1600 BCE, in what tradition remembers as the victory of the founder Tang over the last king of the Xia at the Battle of Mingtiao, a new political order took shape in the middle Yellow River valley of north China. The Shang dynasty, ruled from a succession of walled capitals culminating at Yin near modern Anyang, is the earliest Chinese dynasty firmly attested by archaeology, by cast-bronze ritual vessels, and by the oracle-bone inscriptions that record the earliest known form of Chinese writing. Its five-and-a-half centuries laid the ritual, technological, and literary foundations of Chinese civilisation.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The traditional narrative, preserved in the Records of the Grand Historian compiled by Sima Qian in the first century BCE, tells the story of a virtuous chieftain named Tang, prince of the small state of Shang, who was called by Heaven to overthrow the last Xia king Jie, a tyrant who had ruined his realm with cruelty and drink. At the battle of Mingtiao, dated by tradition to around 1600 BCE, Tang defeated Jie and founded a new dynasty that took its name from his ancestral fief.

For most of the twentieth century Western scholars treated this story as legend. That view began to change in 1899 when the antiquarian Wang Yirong, chancellor of the Imperial Academy in Beijing, noticed strange inscriptions on 'dragon bones' being sold as medicine in the capital, and traced them to the village of Xiaotun near Anyang in Henan. Excavations there from 1928 onward under the newly founded Academia Sinica, led first by Li Chi (Li Ji), uncovered the ruins of a huge Bronze Age capital, later confirmed to be the Great Settlement Shang, the Yin capital of the last nine Shang kings. The oracle bones themselves, tens of thousands recovered, bore inscriptions in an early form of Chinese script that could be read against the king-list preserved in Sima Qian's history. The parallel was striking enough to persuade even sceptical scholars that the Shang was a historical dynasty.

The question of Shang origins is more complicated. The rich urban site of Erlitou in central Henan, occupied from about 1900 to 1500 BCE, is identified by many Chinese archaeologists with the Xia; the succeeding Erligang culture, centred on a massive rammed-earth walled city of some 300 hectares at Zhengzhou and dated to about 1600 to 1400 BCE, is generally taken to represent an early Shang capital. The transition from Erlitou to Erligang, involving new bronze vessel types and a shift of political gravity, corresponds well to the traditional date of Tang's conquest. But the connection between the archaeological cultures and the dynastic labels of Sima Qian remains a matter of interpretation and, in some cases, of vigorous scholarly disagreement.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: A cast-bronze gong (guang) ritual vessel from the tomb of Fu Hao at Anyang, c. 1200 BCE.

sections.body:
What can be said with confidence is that in the sixteenth century BCE a distinctive Bronze Age political order consolidated in the middle Yellow River basin. Its material signature is unmistakable. Piece-mould casting, a technique unique to China at this scale, produced ritual vessels of astonishing complexity: tripod ding cauldrons for cooking meat offerings, gui bowls for grain, jue cups for wine, and later the great zun animal-form vessels of the Yin period. The alloy was a hard bronze of copper, tin, and lead, cast in ceramic moulds assembled in sections around a clay core. The largest surviving Shang vessel, the Houmuwu ding (formerly called the Simuwu ding), cast around 1200 BCE for the mother of King Zu Geng, weighs 832 kilograms and is the heaviest single bronze object ever cast anywhere in the ancient world.

Shang capitals moved several times, whether from defensive necessity, flooding, factional politics, or dynastic renewal. Tradition names five capitals before Pan Geng, the seventeenth Shang king, moved the court to a new site he called Yin (modern Anyang, in northern Henan) around 1300 BCE. Yin remained the capital for the last nine kings, some 250 years, and it is Yin that the twentieth-century excavations at Xiaotun and the neighbouring cemeteries revealed. The city was undefended, a sprawl of palaces, workshops, and burial grounds along a bend in the Huan River. Twelve enormous royal tombs, cruciform shafts sunk more than ten metres into the loess, were built in the western cemetery at Xibeigang; most had been thoroughly looted long before excavation, but their scale (up to a thousand square metres of tomb pit, each with dozens of human sacrifices) gives some idea of the wealth of the late Shang state.

The most complete Shang royal burial ever recovered belonged to Lady Fu Hao, consort of King Wu Ding (r. c. 1250 to 1192 BCE) and, remarkably, a general who led armies of up to 13,000 troops in campaigns to the west. Her intact tomb, discovered by the archaeologist Zheng Zhenxiang at Xiaotun in 1976, contained 468 bronze objects (including 210 ritual vessels), 755 jades, nearly 7,000 cowrie shells, and 16 sacrificed human retainers. It is the only unlooted royal Shang tomb ever found.

Writing appears at Yin in mature form. Some 150,000 fragments of oracle bones, mostly turtle plastrons and cattle scapulae, have been recovered. The Shang kings used them to consult the ancestors on almost every conceivable question, from the outcome of a battle to the meaning of a toothache. A specialist diviner would apply a heated point to a prepared groove on the back of the bone, producing a T-shaped crack on the front; the crack was then interpreted, and the question, answer, and often the verified outcome were engraved in short cuneiform-like graphs. Roughly 4,500 distinct characters have been catalogued, of which perhaps a third can be securely read. The script is unquestionably the ancestor of modern Chinese writing, and no plausible parallel or precursor from outside China has ever been identified.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The Shang state was destroyed by conquest from the west. The Zhou, a semi-peripheral people from the Wei River valley in modern Shaanxi, had for generations acknowledged Shang overlordship while quietly building up their own military strength. In 1046 BCE (the date fixed by the state-sponsored Xia-Shang-Zhou Chronology Project in 2000, though a slightly earlier 1059 BCE is defended by some scholars), King Wu of Zhou led a coalition army against the last Shang king Di Xin (known to hostile Zhou tradition as the tyrant Zhou Xin) at the Battle of Muye, southwest of the Yin capital. Di Xin, whose forces are said in the Zhou-period Shangshu to have refused to fight, was defeated and burnt himself alive in his jade-hung palace. The Shang royal line was extinguished, though a cadet branch was allowed to continue as the small vassal state of Song, which survived until the third century BCE.

The Zhou justified their conquest with a new doctrine: the Mandate of Heaven (tianming). Heaven, they argued, transferred its favour from ruler to ruler on the basis of virtue; the Shang had lost the mandate through the cruelty and impiety of Di Xin, and the Zhou had received it. This doctrine, unknown in the Shang oracle bones but fully articulated in Zhou bronze inscriptions and later texts, would remain the standard theory of political legitimacy in China until the twentieth century.

Materially, the Zhou preserved and elaborated the Shang inheritance. The bronze vessel repertoire, the ancestor cult, the script, the political geography of walled cities in the Yellow River valley, all continued into the Western Zhou period. Human sacrifice, however, declined dramatically; large-scale immolations of war captives, which are estimated at over 13,000 individuals from the surviving Anyang oracle bones alone, are essentially absent from Zhou tombs. Whether this represents a moral revolution or merely a shift in ritual fashion is debated.

sections.heading: Legacy

image.caption: A Shang-dynasty oracle bone: an ox scapula inscribed in early Chinese characters, from Yinxu near modern Anyang.

sections.body:
For subsequent Chinese tradition the Shang was always a slightly ambiguous ancestor. On the one hand, the Zhou classics castigated the last Shang king as a moral warning, and Confucius, whose own descent was traced to the Shang, treated Shang ritual with respect while placing the political ideal earlier, in the semi-mythological Xia. On the other hand, the sheer volume and sophistication of Shang bronzes and jades meant that no serious account of Chinese antiquity could dispense with them. Sima Qian's chapter on the Shang, drawing on temple records and possibly on manuscripts that no longer survive, gives a king-list that has been strikingly confirmed, though not in every detail, by the oracle bones.

The modern recovery of the Shang has been one of the great archaeological projects of the twentieth century. The Academia Sinica campaigns at Anyang from 1928 to 1937, interrupted by the Japanese invasion, established Chinese scientific archaeology as a discipline. Postwar excavations under the People's Republic have added the great Erligang wall at Zhengzhou, the fortified site of Panlongcheng in Hubei, and the extraordinary find at Sanxingdui in Sichuan (roughly contemporary with the late Shang but wholly distinct in style), which revealed that Bronze Age China was more culturally diverse than the traditional dynastic narrative had suggested.

The legacy of the Shang in modern Chinese culture is above all a legacy of script. Every Chinese character carries a genealogy that can, at least in principle, be traced back to an ancestor incised on a Shang oracle bone. The 800 million people who read Chinese today are participants in a continuous literary tradition older than any other on earth, and its earliest chapter was written on burnt bone in the courtyards of Yin.

worldContext.text: In Mesopotamia the great Old Babylonian empire built by Hammurabi a century and a half earlier had collapsed. In 1595 BCE the Hittite king Mursili I made a lightning raid down the Euphrates and sacked Babylon itself, ending the First Dynasty. The Kassites, a people from the Zagros mountains, established a new dynasty that would rule Babylonia for the next four centuries in relative stability but with much less military ambition than the Amorite kings they replaced.

In Egypt this was the age of the Hyksos, Semitic-speaking rulers from the Levant whose Fifteenth Dynasty controlled the eastern Delta from the fortified capital at Avaris. Their introduction of the horse-drawn chariot, the composite bow, and the bronze khopesh sword would reshape Egyptian warfare. The princes of Thebes, notionally ruling Upper Egypt as the Seventeenth Dynasty, were building the coalition that in the mid-sixteenth century BCE, under Ahmose I, would drive the Hyksos out and inaugurate the New Kingdom.

In the Aegean the palace-based Minoan civilisation of Crete was at its late peak. A catastrophic eruption of the Thera (Santorini) volcano, dated by radiocarbon to around 1600 BCE (though with a lively debate over precision), buried the town of Akrotiri under metres of ash and disrupted maritime trade across the eastern Mediterranean. On the Greek mainland the Mycenaean shaft graves at Mycenae, with their gold masks and inlaid daggers, were being cut into the earth.

In the Indus valley the mature Harappan cities were essentially gone. Mohenjo-daro was largely abandoned; Harappa was reduced to a shadow of its former self. In their place a mosaic of smaller cultures, the Late Harappan, the Cemetery H, and various regional traditions, filled the plains. Indo-Aryan speaking pastoralists whose descendants would compose the Rigveda were beginning to move into the northwestern subcontinent from the Central Asian steppe.

In Mesoamerica the era of the great civilisations lay in the future. Villages along the Gulf coast of what is now Veracruz were consolidating into the ceremonial centres that in a few centuries would produce the Olmec. In Peru the coastal and Andean traditions that would culminate in Chavin de Huantar were still in their formative stage, and the great platform mounds of the Norte Chico had been abandoned four centuries earlier.

The Austronesian expansion out of Taiwan was under way. Speakers of proto-Malayo-Polynesian languages had reached the northern Philippines around 2200 BCE and were moving southward through Indonesia. The Lapita cultural complex, whose beautifully dentate-stamped pottery would mark the first colonisation of Remote Oceania, was still a few centuries away in the Bismarck Archipelago. Australia continued to be inhabited by its established populations of hunter-gatherer peoples.

figures.name: Tang of Shang
figures.role: Traditional founder of the Shang dynasty, whose victory over the Xia king Jie at Mingtiao is placed around 1600 BCE

figures.name: Pan Geng
figures.role: Seventeenth Shang king, who around 1300 BCE moved the capital to Yin (modern Anyang), which remained the seat of the dynasty until its fall

figures.name: Wu Ding
figures.role: Twenty-first Shang king (r. c. 1250 to 1192 BCE) whose long reign is exceptionally well documented in the oracle-bone archives

figures.name: Fu Hao
figures.role: Consort of Wu Ding and a Shang military commander, whose intact tomb at Anyang was discovered in 1976
figures.credit: Statue at Fu Hao's tomb, Anyang, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Di Xin (Zhou Xin)
figures.role: Last Shang king, defeated by King Wu of Zhou at the Battle of Muye in 1046 BCE

images.caption: A Shang-dynasty oracle bone: an ox scapula inscribed in early Chinese characters with a divination record, from Yinxu, near modern Anyang.
A cast-bronze gong (guang) ritual vessel from the tomb of Fu Hao at Anyang, c. 1200 BCE. Henan Provincial Museum, Zhengzhou.
